title: The practice of letting go of the identity of being broken
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You've been wearing broken like a coat

I know what it's like to wake up and check. To scan your body for the familiar ache, the familiar tightness, the familiar story. Still here. Still broken. Still the person who can't quite get it right. I spent years believing that broken was my permanent address. Not a temporary rental. Not a phase. My identity. Here's the thing: your nervous system doesn't know the difference between a temporary feeling and a permanent label. It just knows what's familiar. And when you've been traumatized, familiarity feels like safety. Even when that familiarity is pain.

So you keep showing up as the broken one. The one who can't handle conflict. The one who dissociates in the middle of conversations. The one who apologizes for existing. Your friends know you that way. Your family knows you that way. You know you that way. And letting go of that identity? It feels like dying. Because in a way, it is. The person you've been surviving as has to make room for someone you don't know yet.

I remember the exact moment I realized I'd been performing brokenness. I was in a therapy session, and my therapist asked me a simple question: "If you weren't the person who's been hurt, who would you be?" I couldn't answer. My mind went blank. Because I had no reference point for a version of me that wasn't organized around my wounds. That's when I understood what Bessel van der Kolk meant when he wrote that trauma changes the very structure of who we are. It's not just in your memories. It's in your posture, your breathing, your expectations of other people, your relationship with your own body.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman wrote it decades ago, and it's still the clearest map I've found for understanding how trauma reorganizes your sense of self. She talks about how survivors often develop a "contaminated identity" - the belief that you are damaged, that the trauma has seeped into your bones and become you. That's the identity I'm talking about. The one that whispers "this is just who you are now" every time you try to imagine a different life.

The nervous system doesn't do identity politics

Here's what took me years to learn: your nervous system doesn't care about your identity. It cares about survival. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that the autonomic nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. It's not making philosophical arguments about who you are. It's asking one question: "Am I safe right now?" And if the answer is no, it mobilizes you for fight or flight, or it immobilizes you into shutdown and collapse.

Think about it. When you're in a triggered state, do you think "I am a broken person"? Or do you just feel the panic, the numbness, the urge to escape, the need to disappear? The identity story comes after. It's a narrative your brain constructs to make sense of the physical experience. But the physical experience itself? That's just your nervous system doing its job. Trying to protect you from a threat it believes is still present.

So when I talk about letting go of the identity of being broken, I'm not asking you to pretend you're fine. I'm not asking you to slap a positive affirmation on a wound that hasn't healed. I'm asking you to notice the difference between what your body feels and what your mind tells you about what your body feels. Stay with me here. This is subtle. This is the work.

Your body might feel heavy. Your chest might be tight. Your throat might be constricted. Those are sensations. They are real. They are valid. They deserve compassion. But the story "I am broken because I feel this way"? That's a narrative. And narratives can be rewritten. Not by force. Not by denial. But by gentle, repeated observation.

I started practicing this by sitting with my sensations without immediately attaching a meaning to them. I would notice the tightness in my jaw and just say "tightness." Not "I'm tense because I'm damaged." Not "I can't relax because I'm broken." Just "tightness." And then I would breathe. And then I would notice what happened next. Sometimes the tightness stayed. Sometimes it shifted. Sometimes it got worse before it got better. But every time I resisted the urge to narrate it into an identity, I was practicing something radical: I was letting my body have its experience without turning that experience into a permanent statement about who I am.

The parts that believe you're broken are just trying to protect you

Richard Schwartz developed Internal Family Systems, and his work changed how I understand the parts of myself that cling to brokenness. He talks about how the parts of us that seem the most damaged, the most stuck, the most hopeless are actually protectors. They took on a role early in life to help you survive. The part that believes you're broken? It's probably a part that learned that if you expect the worst, you won't be disappointed. If you identify as someone who can't succeed, you won't take risks that could lead to failure. If you're the one who's always hurting, no one can hurt you more than you've already hurt yourself. Wild, right? The very identity that feels like a prison is actually a protection strategy your system developed to keep you safe.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Schwartz walks you through the idea that there are no bad parts, only parts that are trying to help in ways that might not be helpful anymore. When I first read that, I cried. Because I had been trying to get rid of my broken parts. I had been fighting them, shaming them, trying to exorcise them. But they weren't my enemies. They were exhausted, terrified children who had been doing a job no one should have to do.

So the practice of letting go of the identity of being broken isn't about getting rid of the parts that feel broken. It's about getting to know them. Asking them what they're afraid would happen if you stopped believing you were broken. Asking them how old they think you are. Asking them what they need from you now, as an adult, that they didn't get when they were formed.

I asked my "broken" part what it needed. It said it needed me to stop trying to fix it. It needed me to sit with it without agenda. It needed me to acknowledge that it had kept me alive through things no one should have to survive. And then it said something that shattered me: "I don't know who I am if I'm not protecting you. I'm scared." That's when I realized that letting go of the broken identity wasn't just my work. It was our work. Me and all the parts of me that had been carrying this story for decades.

Grieving the identity you're leaving behind

Letting go of brokenness involves grief. Real, messy, uncomfortable grief. Because that identity, as painful as it was, was familiar. It was known. It had a shape and a texture and a predictable rhythm. You knew how to be broken. You knew what was expected of you. You knew the rules. When you start to step out of that identity, you lose your script. You lose your excuse. You lose the explanation for why your life looks the way it does.

I grieved my broken identity for months. I didn't even know that's what I was doing at first. I just felt this strange emptiness, this disorientation, this sense of having lost something even though I was supposedly getting better. A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises that helped me name what I was losing and honor it. Because you can't just drop an identity. You have to thank it for its service. You have to acknowledge what it cost you to carry it. You have to let it rest.

Peter Levine talks about pendulation in Somatic Experiencing - the natural rhythm of moving between discomfort and ease, between contraction and expansion. I think identity change works the same way. You don't just one day stop being broken. You move toward wholeness, then you swing back into old patterns. You feel the grief of losing who you were, then you feel the terror of not knowing who you're becoming. You take a step forward, then you need to rest. That's not failure. That's the rhythm of healing.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helped me understand why I developed a broken identity in the first place. When your emotional needs aren't met as a child, you internalize that as something being wrong with you. You become the problem so you can maintain the illusion that your caregivers are safe. Letting go of that identity means facing the truth: you weren't broken as a child. You were adapting to an environment that wasn't safe enough for you to be whole.

Who are you without the story?

I'm not going to give you a neat answer to that question. Because I don't have one. And honestly, anyone who tells you they know exactly who they are after letting go of their trauma identity is probably selling something. The truth is, you might not know for a while. You might feel like a stranger in your own life. You might try on different versions of yourself and find that none of them fit quite right. That's okay. That's the process.

What I can tell you is what I've noticed in my own body since I started this work. I notice more space. More room to breathe. Less of that constant tension that was so familiar I didn't even recognize it as tension. I notice that when something hard happens, I'm more likely to feel it as a wave passing through rather than a permanent state of being. I notice that I'm less interested in proving I'm okay and more interested in actually being okay.

Janina Fisher, who wrote about treating the traumatized self, talks about how the goal isn't to get rid of your trauma responses. The goal is to have a relationship with them. To be able to say "there's the part that feels broken" instead of "I am broken." That tiny shift in language changes everything. It creates space between you and the experience. And in that space, something new can grow.

So here's my invitation to you. Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Notice if there's a part of you that wants to hold on to brokenness. Notice if there's a part that's terrified of letting it go. Notice if there's a part that's already rolling its eyes at this whole article. All of those parts are welcome. All of them have something to say. And none of them are the whole truth about who you are.

You are not your trauma. You are not your diagnosis. You are not the story you've been telling yourself about why you can't heal. You are the one who has been surviving all of this, and that survival has taken immense strength. But survival isn't the same as living. And you deserve more than survival. You deserve to find out who you are when you're not trying to prove you're broken.

Sit with that. Let it land. And then take the next small step toward something that isn't quite broken, isn't quite whole, but is somehow more real than either of those labels could ever capture.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
